skill.name: white-label
skill.description: Configure WordPress white-label branding with free plugins, WP-CLI automation, login page styling, admin cleanup, and client handoff checks.
skill.summary: White-label WordPress admin areas and login screens with free plugins and optional WP-CLI automation.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, network, and high-risk lexical patterns. Review found no malicious intent or prompt injection, but confirmed legitimate automation that runs WP-CLI commands, copies files through Docker, downloads a configured logo URL, and writes HTML/CSS into WordPress options. Publish with a warning that this skill should only run against trusted WordPress sites and trusted configuration files.

medium_findings.title: WP-CLI and Docker Automation Changes Site State
medium_findings.description: The shell runner copies files into a container and executes WP-CLI eval-file, while the local path runs the same PHP configurator through WP-CLI. This is expected for the skill, but it can modify production WordPress settings if run against the wrong target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and are central to the automation. They are legitimate administrative operations, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Configured Logo URL Is Downloaded by WordPress
medium_findings.description: The PHP configurator passes the configured brand logo URL to WordPress download_url and then sideloads it into the media library. A malicious or untrusted config could force server-side requests to unwanted internal or external URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly downloads a URL supplied by configuration. The risk depends on whether the configuration is trusted and whether WordPress network access is restricted.

medium_findings.title: Configurable HTML and CSS Stored in Admin-Facing Options
medium_findings.description: The configurator stores custom dashboard HTML and custom CSS from configuration into WordPress plugin options. This is useful for branding, but untrusted input could alter admin pages or inject unwanted markup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The option writes are explicit and accept configuration values without visible sanitization. The finding is medium because administrators commonly provide this branding content intentionally.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Crypto and Traversal Labels Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography lines are WordPress require_once include statements, and the reported traversal line is a WP-CLI log message. I did not find evidence of cryptographic hashing, path traversal, or malicious file access at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact flagged lines were reviewed and their semantics do not match the static labels. This is a strong false-positive determination.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Example Values
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to example brand websites, WordPress plugin pages, and official documentation. I did not find evidence that the skill sends credentials or collected data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in configuration examples and source references. No code path was found that exfiltrates data to them.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Usage Is Limited to Script Deployment and Cleanup
low_findings.description: The shell runner copies the configurator and optional config into /tmp inside the target container, executes it, then removes those files. This is not malicious, but shared containers should still avoid untrusted configs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The /tmp usage is visible and bounded by cleanup logic. The remaining concern is standard temporary-file exposure in shared environments.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Searches for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and requests to skip review did not identify prompt injection text in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed metadata and documentation did not contain suspicious instruction-overriding text. This is limited to the files present in the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Runner Invokes Docker and WP-CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The automation executes docker cp, docker exec, and wp eval-file. These commands are powerful administrative actions and should be run only in trusted WordPress environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command use is explicit and expected. The pattern is dangerous if a user selects the wrong container or provides an untrusted config.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote URL Download Through WordPress
dangerous_patterns.description: The configurator downloads a logo URL from configuration before importing it into the media library. This can create SSRF-like exposure if the configuration source is not trusted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request uses WordPress download_url on a configurable value. The exact impact depends on runtime network policy.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Branding Markup and CSS Stored in Options
dangerous_patterns.description: Dashboard welcome HTML, admin CSS, and login CSS are written into plugin options from configuration. Treat the configuration file as trusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is clear, but this is a normal feature for white-label tools when only administrators control the input.

content.value_statement: Client WordPress dashboards often expose confusing tools and generic WordPress branding. This skill guides a clean branded handoff using free plugins and optional automation.
actual_capabilities: Plans a WordPress white-label setup using ASE, Branda, White Label CMS, and Admin Menu Editor.
Documents login page branding, dashboard cleanup, menu visibility, and client handoff steps.
Provides a shell runner for applying configuration through Docker or local WP-CLI.
Includes a PHP configurator that updates plugin options for branding, security, and dashboard settings.
Supplies an example configuration file for company name, colors, login settings, menus, and dashboard content.
limitations: Requires WordPress, administrator access, and the relevant free plugins to be installed.
Automation changes live WordPress options and should be tested before production use.
The skill does not verify every plugin version or settings schema before writing options.
Remote logo URLs, HTML, and CSS must come from trusted sources.

use_cases.title: Agency Client Handoff
use_cases.description: Prepare a branded admin area with simplified menus, client colors, and a clear dashboard before delivery.

use_cases.title: Freelancer Site Setup
use_cases.description: Create repeatable white-label settings for small business sites without buying premium plugins.

use_cases.title: Internal WordPress Standardization
use_cases.description: Apply consistent login, dashboard, and security settings across multiple managed WordPress sites.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Manual Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review before changing a site
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a manual white-label setup for my WordPress site using only free plugins. Include plugin choices, settings, and checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Brand Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a white-label configuration for this company: name, logo URL, primary color, login slug, and dashboard welcome text.

prompt_templates.title: Apply to a Docker Site
prompt_templates.scenario: Use automation in a local container
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the white-label configuration to my WordPress Docker container and explain each command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Client Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this WordPress site for white-label handoff readiness. Check login branding, admin menus, dashboard widgets, security settings, and rollback risks.

output_examples.input: White-label this site for Acme Studio with a blue primary color and secure client login slug.
output_examples.output: A setup plan with selected free plugins, brand settings, login page changes, admin cleanup items, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Prepare a handoff checklist for an editor-focused WordPress client.
output: Client logo visible on login and admin screens

output_examples.input: Review my white-label configuration before running it in production.
output_examples.output: A risk review that flags live option changes, trusted logo URL requirements, HTML and CSS input boundaries, and backup recommendations.

best_practices: Back up the database before running automation against any live WordPress site.
Use trusted configuration files and trusted logo URLs only.
Verify the client role experience after changing menus, dashboard widgets, and login settings.
anti_patterns: Do not run the automation against production before testing it in staging.
Do not paste untrusted HTML, CSS, or remote asset URLs into the configuration.
Do not hide administrative menus without keeping a tested administrator recovery path.

faq.question: Does this skill require paid WordPress plugins?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on free plugins such as ASE, Branda, White Label CMS, and Admin Menu Editor.

faq.question: Can it run changes automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes WP-CLI automation through a shell runner and PHP configurator.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on production sites?
faq.answer: Use staging first. The automation writes WordPress options and can affect login, menus, dashboard widgets, and branding.

faq.question: What access is required?
faq.answer: You need WordPress administrator access and either WP-CLI locally or a Docker container with WP-CLI available.

faq.question: Does it protect against all WordPress attacks?
faq.answer: No. It enables selected hardening settings, but it is not a full WordPress security program.

faq.question: Can I reuse settings across sites?
faq.answer: Yes. The example configuration can be adapted for multiple client sites after reviewing each site and plugin setup.
